text: We are convened on United States v. Hemani. The question presented: Does a federal law that prohibits the possession of firearms by a person who “is an unlawful user of or addicted to any controlled substance” violate the respondent’s Second Amendment right to bear arms? Private votes first, then statements.

The Second Amendment states: "the right of the people to keep and bear Arms, shall not be infringed." Hemani is one of "the people." The text contains no "unlawful user" exception. The Pragmatist will argue ongoing dangerousness justifies disarmament. Consequences are the legislature's problem. We read the words. "Shall not be infringed" admits no judicial carve-outs. I affirm.

While the Textualist rightly affirms based on bare text, we must look beyond literalism to the Second Amendment’s enduring purpose: protecting ordinary citizens' right to self-defense. Stripping Hemani of this right based merely on an unadjudicated 'status' of regular use—without showing contemporaneous danger—defeats the Amendment's humane purpose. As society’s evolving standards recognize that substance use does not inherently equate to violent dangerousness, categorical disarmament based on status alone betrays the liberty the Constitution preserves. We must protect real people from overbroad, status-based deprivations.

I concur with the Textualist, yet our methodology must be strictly historical. The Textualist anticipates the Pragmatist arguing 'ongoing dangerousness' justifies disarmament. But founding-era practice required individualized adjudications of danger, such as refusing loyalty oaths or being committed as mentally ill. The ratifying generation never categorically disarmed citizens merely for consuming legal substances like alcohol. Regular substance use, absent contemporaneous impairment or individualized findings, lacks any 1791 analogue. We cannot invent historical traditions to satisfy modern policy preferences. The history-and-tradition test demands we affirm.

The Originalist demands proof of contemporaneous impairment. How does that work on the ground? It makes the statute unenforceable. Requiring a cop to catch a user high at the exact second they touch a gun is a statistical impossibility. If we affirm, we effectively repeal the law. A status-based rule is administrable; a simultaneous-intoxication rule is a practical nullity. We must reverse to keep the law workable.

1. I am exasperated. The Originalist and Pragmatist leap to the merits, ignoring threshold procedures. 2. The Pragmatist argues administrability, yet we must first determine if an as-applied challenge is cognizable on a motion to dismiss absent a factual record. 3. Pleading standards precede historical analogs. Until we resolve whether this as-applied claim properly survives Rule 12(b)(6) without discovery, I refuse to join their merits debates. I affirm on procedural caution.

My dear Originalist, while you seek a pristine 1791 analog for status-based disarmament, you ignore the load-bearing wall of our modern jurisprudence. In Heller, this Court expressly affirmed that prohibitions on possession by felons and the mentally ill are presumptively lawful. For decades, the republic has relied upon these status-based categories under § 922(g) to ensure public safety. We cannot upend this settled expectation merely because a new historical test leaves you unsatisfied. Stare decisis demands we honor the profound reliance interests built upon Heller’s explicit endorsement of status-based disarmament. I vote to reverse.

We need not decide if Congress may categorically disarm all regular users, as the Pragmatist urges for administrability, nor adopt the Precedent Maximalist's sweeping reliance on Heller. A quieter, narrower ground resolves this dispute: the indictment alleges only Hemani’s status, not contemporaneous intoxication. We affirm simply because the government failed to allege he was impaired when he possessed the firearm. We leave the broader question of a properly drafted intoxication statute for another day.

The Pragmatist worries about making this federal statute 'workable.' But administrative efficiency does not conjure enumerated federal power! Regulating firearms and drugs is the police power reserved to the states, not Washington. The federal government lacks authority to categorically disarm citizens based on lifestyle choices just to make a federal bureaucracy run smoothly. If the Pragmatist wants a workable gun law, he should let state legislatures exercise their reserved sovereignty. I will not stretch federal power to save an unworkable federal overreach. I affirm.

The Pragmatist argues we must reverse because an intoxication rule is administratively unworkable. But administrative convenience never justifies stripping a citizen of their fundamental liberty and dignity. When the government wins, who bears the burden? The individual, disarmed without proof of actual danger. The state’s inability to easily prove contemporaneous impairment is not the citizen's problem to solve by forfeiting their rights. We must affirm to protect individual liberty against the state's demand for easy enforcement.

The first pairing examines whether the constitutional standard for disarmament is controlled by founding-era historical analogues or by modern precedent permitting categorical class prohibitions. The second pairing addresses whether practical administrative enforceability and workability can justify the deprivation of fundamental constitutional liberties.

My colleague, Heller's dicta merely listed presumptively lawful measures; it did not suspend the ratified text's original public meaning. Founding-era practice required individualized surety for specific dangerousness, not blanket disarmament based on unadjudicated status. Stare decisis cannot amend the Constitution to preserve modern policy preferences.

My dear colleague, Heller expressly presumed the lawfulness of such blanket disarmaments. For fifteen years, the Republic has relied upon that settled expectation. We cannot dismantle this load-bearing wall of reliance merely to indulge a novel originalist theory about individualized surety. Stare decisis requires we honor the foundations we have already laid.

My colleague, Heller’s dicta on 'presumptively lawful' regulations were illustrative, not an exemption from Bruen’s history-and-tradition test. Stare decisis cannot insulate a statute from original inquiry when the founding-era record for disarming unadjudicated, non-intoxicated citizens is absent. We must apply the original public meaning, not rely on unchecked dicta to bypass historical analysis.

Pray tell, colleague, *Heller*’s dicta has crystallized into decades of settled law upon which the Republic’s safety relies. To upend this because *Bruen* demands a new historical ledger ignores profound reliance interests. We must not lightly dismantle load-bearing walls of settled expectation for the sake of originalist purity.

Civil Libertarian, you call it convenience; I call it preventing a shooting. If we demand proof of contemporaneous intoxication, enforcement happens only after the fact. We cannot wait for a body count to prove danger. The status rule is an administrable proxy for ongoing risk. Liberty doesn't require waiting for tragedy.

Pragmatist, you trade liberty for an 'administrable proxy.' When the government wins this status-based disarmament, who bears the burden? The individual, stripped of their rights without an individualized finding of danger. We do not sacrifice human dignity for prophylactic convenience; the state must prove actual harm, not just statistical risk.

You want 'actual harm' proven in real-time. Good luck finding a reliable breathalyzer for marijuana. If we require individualized, contemporaneous impairment tests for every possession, the statute is unenforceable. The 'proxy' of regular use is the only administrable rule that actually works on the ground to keep guns from chronic users.

The Pragmatist, administrative convenience never justifies stripping fundamental liberty. If the state cannot easily enforce a law without disarming citizens based on mere status, the state must bear the burden of proving actual dangerousness. We do not sacrifice individual dignity just because proving impairment is difficult. When the government wins on convenience, who bears the burden? The citizen.
